mnemonics: SET and RESET are like a light switch: SET turns it ON (1), RESET turns it OFF (0).
CLK is the boss: Changes only happen on the boss's command (clock edge).
D stands for Data: Whatever Data you put in, is what you'll get out on the next clock tick.
keyPoints: Flip-flops are edge-triggered, meaning they only change state on a specific clock edge (rising or falling).
The D flip-flop (Data) is the most common type and captures the value of its D input at the moment of the clock edge.
Flip-flops have two stable states (0 and 1), which is what makes them the basic building block for memory.
commonMistakes: Confusing latches (level-sensitive) with flip-flops (edge-triggered). A latch is transparent while enable is high; a flip-flop only acts on a clock edge.
Forgetting the setup and hold time requirements, which dictate how long the data must be stable before and after the clock edge to be reliably captured.
Assuming a flip-flop's output changes immediately after its input changes, when in reality the output only updates on the next valid clock edge.
